title: The Best Movies of 2020 … So Far
format: Ringer critic Adam Nayman joins Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins to trade top-five lists of the best movies of 2020 so far, and to talk through what watching movies at home instead of in theaters has changed about how they see them.

segments.heading: Movies at home
segments.note: on watching everything on a screen that isn't a movie screen

films.title: The King of Staten Island
films.note: Amanda didn't watch it until Sunday afternoon of opening weekend because the Judd Apatow runtime needed eight free hours. Adam, who wrote about it for the site, says nothing about it demands a big screen, but the habit of seeing things on opening weekend hasn't left him.

segments.heading: Adam's Top Five
segments.subject: the best movies of 2020, so far

films.title: The Assistant
films.note: No. 1. Kitty Green's minimalist portrait of a production assistant's day in a Miramax-like office. Adam says the first fifteen minutes play like an abstract film about being the first person into the office, and the Matthew Macfadyen phone call does the work of explaining the whole world in one scene.

films.title: The Vast of Night
films.note: No. 2, shared with Sean. Andrew Patterson's Twilight Zone-inflected sci-fi debut, spotted by Soderbergh at Slamdance and released on Amazon. Adam has watched it three times and compares its confidence to Spielberg's Sugarland Express.

films.title: First Cow
films.note: No. 3, shared with Sean. Kelly Reichardt's frontier friendship film. Adam calls her "the queen of subtlety" and hopes an Oscar nomination might finally widen the audience for a filmmaker who's gone fifteen years mostly ignored outside the Independent Spirit Awards.

films.title: Bacurau
films.note: No. 4. Kleber Mendonça Filho and Juliano Dornelles' Brazilian village-under-siege film. Adam calls it his version of Assault on Precinct 13 with a little They Live mixed in, and saw it at TIFF with a whooping audience.

films.title: Vitalina Varela
films.note: No. 5. Pedro Costa's hushed drama, cast with non-professionals, about a widow returning to her hometown. Adam saw it at TIFF in a pin-drop screening and says it's the pick that makes him miss theaters most, though he still recommends watching it at home.

segments.heading: Amanda's Top Five
segments.subject: the best movies of 2020, so far

films.title: Never Rarely Sometimes Always
films.note: No. 1, shared with Sean. Eliza Hittman's drama follows a Pennsylvania teenager traveling to New York for an abortion. Amanda calls it issue-driven but not preachy, carried by what she calls an incredible performance from Sidney Flanigan.

films.title: The Assistant
films.note: No. 2, shared with Adam (his No. 1). Amanda points to the Matthew Macfadyen phone-call scene as the moment the film "grabs you."

films.title: On the Record
films.note: No. 3. Kirby Dick and Amy Ziering's documentary on the allegations against Russell Simmons, centered on former Def Jam A&R executive Drew Dixon. Amanda singles out a roughly fifteen-minute passage on what going public means for Dixon as a Black woman.

films.title: Da 5 Bloods
films.note: No. 4. Spike Lee's Netflix Vietnam-vet drama. Amanda calls Delroy Lindo's performance the performance of the year, and says she's gone back to rewatch his climactic jungle monologue on its own.

films.title: The Half of It
films.note: No. 5. Alice Wu's queer teen adaptation of Cyrano de Bergerac, Wu's first film since 2004's Saving Face. Amanda calls it proof a Netflix teen movie doesn't have to be the lowest-common-denominator version.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five
segments.subject: the best movies of 2020, so far

films.title: The Way Back
films.note: No. 4. Gavin O'Connor's drama stars Ben Affleck as a washed-up ex-college basketball star coaching his old high school team. Sean pitches it as Bad News Bears meets The Lost Weekend and says it's one of the last movies he saw in a theater before lockdown.

films.title: The Invisible Man
films.note: No. 5. Leigh Whannell's Blumhouse reboot with Elisabeth Moss. Sean calls it the last movie that gave him a physical reaction in a theater, saying he went from nervous to excited to laughing.
